metric_value: 40% average; up to 70% in some cases
notes: Aberdeen research: enterprise IT budget committed to application integration averages 40%, rising to 70% in some situations.
Nike's SAP+Siebel+i2 integration initiative: 4-year, $400M endeavor that led to missed quarterly earnings.
Aberdeen observed 2-3 year lag between stand-alone 3rd-party app availability and integrated suite vendors delivering equivalent functionality.

metric_value: Up to 3 additional years after release
notes: After integrated suite vendors release equivalent functionality, it typically takes up to 3 more years to reach functional parity with point solution.

metric_value: 3-4x license fees to reach equivalent integration
notes: Aberdeen: point solution typically requires initial integration investment equal to 3-4x license fee to reach equivalent integration level.

metric_value: Up to 10x license fees in some cases
notes: Numerous ERP implementations where implementation+integration combined rose to as high as 10x the license fee.
Integration cost drops to 1x license fee for new application delivered as part of an integrated suite.
Beneficial Life (Oracle customer): more than 50% of IT staff involved in new product customization including integration.
Beneficial Life: when very close match in data models/APIs/architecture, integration cost is very close to cost of software itself.
Beneficial Life: when 3rd-party software is distinctly different from company's architecture, integration cost seen as 2-3x cost of software.
Comparative implementation timeline: 3-6 months for one point solution vs. 18-24 months for entire integrated enterprise suite.
Integrating 4 point applications can take 16-20 months — approaching or exceeding time to implement full ERP suite.
Full integrated enterprise suite implementation: 18-24 months.
Ariba, i2, and IBM SCM+e-Procurement partnership launched with million-dollar ad campaign; dissolved in under 6 months, leaving customers with unpredictable costs.
Indian Motorcycle (mid-market): implemented full Oracle suite in 1999, reported achieving ROI within one year of going into production.

Michael Porter (HBR March 2001): nearly every company uses same Internet applications; resulting improvements broadly shared; very rarely can companies gain durable advantages from best-of-breed deployment.

New Economy Darwinism fear drove enterprises to adopt point solutions in late 1990s; Aberdeen concludes this fear proved unrealized and advantages dissipated as integrated suite vendors replicated and improved functionality.

metric_value: Integrated suite recommended for core processes
notes: Aberdeen conclusion: highest degree of integration stems from single inclusive solution — traditional ERP vendors likely winners in long run; Oracle provides such suite with comprehensive best-practice processes.

metric_value: Vendor management skill gap in IT
notes: IT directors report: managing multiple vendor relationships is a unique skill not typically found among IT staff; often not considered in point solution purchase decisions.

metric_value: Future ease of integration promised by XML+OOT
notes: Aberdeen: future holds promise of eased application integration through OOT and XML standards adherence, supported by major suppliers including Microsoft, Oracle, SAP.

metric_value: Common data objects coded differently across point solutions
notes: Example: customer ID '3M' in order entry vs. 'Minnesota Mining and Manufacturing' in purchasing system — real work required to integrate data across point solutions.

metric_value: Competitive advantage dissipates rapidly as others adopt
notes: Bandwagon effect: rapid dissipation of competitive advantage as other enterprises adopt same point application, flattening perceived competitive value.

metric_value: 7 to 1,500 IT staff; midsize to large
notes: Companies interviewed: midsize to large North American firms; IT staffs ranging from 7 to 1,500 employees; majority desired IT environment simplification.
